text: on the notion of ‘contagion’ in his classification 4 There is no indication that Tarde had read Kierkegaard. Yet, their critical analysis of communication and intersubjectivity in the ‘public’ (Tarde) or the ‘crowd’ (Kierkegaard 1964) share important affinities. Liminality and the Modern 102 and analysis of rites. The notion of ‘contagious rites’ is one of the subspecies recognized by van Gennep (1960: 7–14). Van Gennep noticed how many rites involve the belief that natural or acquired characteristics are material and transmissible, either through physical contact or over a distance (ibid.: 7). It is for this reason that neophytes, while in the liminal state, are in most cultural contexts considered dangerous and surrounded by a very explicit taboo prohibiting any physical contact between these liminars and people who stand outside the liminal. This is one of the aspects of rites of passage that is crucial to consider and further develop when applied to periods of transition in large-scale societies. The notion of contagion of course has strong links to the Tardean core notion of imitation: how we imitate, who we imitate and under what circumstances. As is well-known, those questions lie at the heart of the anthropology of René Girard, the main contemporary theorist of the mimetic aspect of human conduct. The starting point is the intuition, radically different from the perspective shared both by traditional associational psychology and Freudian approaches, that desire is primarily elicited by the object. According to Girard, quite on the contrary, desire is primarily mimetic: subject B desires an object, living or inanimate, in the sense of wanting to possess it fully and exclusively as it is already desired by subject A. B is only imitating A’s desire. The outcome of this ‘mimetic desire’, inevitably, is conflict, especially if desire has sexual aspects. This conflict leads to violence and can very easily escalate, imitatively, until it evolves into a full-scale civil war. Rituals and theatrical representations do not therefore simply imitate real acts, but are imitative of already imitative behaviour, and thus have a genuinely cathartic but also dangerous potential. In the last years of his life, Victor Turner came to recognize the theoretical importance of Girard (see for example Turner 1988: 34) and in the precise context of the ritual structure: crisis is contagious, and sometimes the ‘redressive machinery ... fails to function’ (ibid.: 35), leading to ‘a reversion to crisis’. Exploring what he would term ‘public liminality’, Turner also made increasing use of Bateson’s work on play and frame, and Bateson’s notion of ‘metacommunication’. In short, Turner became acutely aware of the imitative aspect of liminal situations, emphasizing that the middle phase of a ritual represented a mimetic enactment of a crisis, while Bateson similarly recognized the imitative, even mimic nature of the Naven ritual (Bateson 1958). In fact, as we shall argue below, his crucial notion of schismogenesis is nothing but a model of escalating imitative behaviour. Girard considered Turner and Bateson as close intellectual allies. According to Girard (1979), imitation plays a vital role in provoking and escalating violence. In fact, acts of violence, together with sexuality and laughter, belong to the most imitative aspects of human behaviour, where the performance of an act almost irresistibly leads to its repetitive imitation, until a certain kind of climax is reached. Girard’s work can here be combined with the perspective of van Gennep and Turner, as liminal situations are particularly conducive to mimesis and the escalation of violence; a phenomenon described by Girard as a ‘mimetic crisis’. Dimensions of Liminality 103 But how does such a system develop that legitimates and institutionalizes violence at the heart of social relations? Girard offers here his theory of scapegoating and the sacrificial mechanism. According to this theory, once the spiralling movement of escalating conflict and violence has started, there is only one possible solution: an innocent must be identified as guilty, excluded and killed, and in this way unanimity can be restored in society. In this sense, Girard sees sacrifice as the foundation of culture. There is every reason to reject this position as a general theory. 5 And yet it is certainly the case that sacrifice and the sacrificial mechanism can become a foundational element of cultural contexts, including perhaps ours the modern one. The general point to stress is this one: imitation poses particular problems in liminal situations, for in the absence of masters of ceremony, who should we follow, and who can take the lead out of the crisis? This takes us to the contrast between charismatic leaders and tricksters. Trickster Makes the World It must be recognized that he [Hitler] possesses a supreme dexterity in juxtaposing contradictions and imposing on his ‘subjects’ composite terms that, since they have no meaning, permit them to think anything. He reminds me of that celebrated ethnopsychologist Wilhelm Wundt, who, when he found it impossible to distinguish between Mythus (myth) and Märchen (folktales), invented the terms Mythusmärchen and its opposite, Märchenmythus, which denote neither the one nor the other yet somehow denote both simultaneously while also distinguishing between them without actually doing so. Hitler must certainly have read Wundt. None of which, however, diminishes his personal magnetism, increases the intelligence of his followers, or decreases our obligation to regard his movement very seriously, if not with dread. After which, perhaps, the Germans may find out what it means to be free. (Arnold van Gennep, in Mercure de France, as translated in Belmont) If rites of passage contain a chaotic, void-like liminal stage, then there must be something or somebody standing outside this void to guide people out of it. In anthropology, this figure is called the master of ceremonies. But what happens in liminal situations when there is no designated master of ceremonies? 5 Girard frequently refers to Mauss and Hertz’s 1898 essay on sacrifice; however, it was Mauss himself who came to realize the problematic aspect of this account which gave genealogical primacy to sacrifice.
